# **Deterministic Transformation of Biographical Narratives into Tabular Schemata: A Text-to-KG-to-Table Pipeline Utilizing Embedding-Driven Filtering**

The systematic extraction of structured data from unstructured biographical texts represents a significant challenge in computational linguistics, specifically when applied to large-scale resources such as the WikiBio dataset. Traditional information extraction (IE) efforts have increasingly pivoted toward generative approaches facilitated by Large Language Models (LLMs); however, the inherent risks of hallucination, the lack of structural transparency, and the prohibitive computational costs of autoregressive decoding have catalyzed a return to deterministic, non-generative frameworks. A robust Text-to-KG-to-Table pipeline must operate through three distinct phases: the deterministic extraction of semantic primitives, the topological representation of these primitives within a Knowledge Graph (KG), and the subsequent pruning and normalization of this graph into a tabular format. By prioritizing person-centric data filtering and leveraging node and edge embeddings for information grouping, it is possible to achieve high-fidelity structured outputs without the need for LLM prompting, multimodal inputs, or Graph Neural Networks (GNNs).

## **The WikiBio Dataset as a Benchmark for Structured Extraction**

The WikiBio dataset, originally introduced by Lebret et al. in 2016, serves as the primary evaluation environment for this pipeline.1 Comprising 728,321 biographies from the English Wikipedia, the dataset pairs the first sentence of each article with a corresponding infobox—a set of subject-attribute-value triples representing the article's summary.3 While originally designed for table-to-text generation, the inversion of this task—text-to-table—presents unique difficulties due to the divergent nature of the source data. Biographies often contain narrative context that is absent from the infobox, and conversely, infoboxes frequently include metadata (such as standardized birth dates or internal identifiers) not explicitly stated in the natural language text.6

| Statistical Attribute | WikiBio Dataset (Mean/Total) | Comparison: RotoWire Dataset |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Total Biography Samples | 728,321 | 4,853 |
| Tokens per Reference Description | 26.1 | 642.0 |
| Average Infobox Token Count | 53.1 | 92.0 |
| Distinct Fields per Infobox | 19.7 | 38.0 |
| Infobox Tokens per Reference | 9.5 | N/A |
| Training Set Volume | 582,660 | 3,398 |

Research indicates that the brevity of WikiBio descriptions (averaging 26.1 tokens) requires a highly precise extraction mechanism that can distinguish between the "target-entity"—the subject of the biography—and peripheral entities mentioned in the same context.2 This person-centric focus is critical, as the resulting table must strictly represent the attributes of the biography's subject.

## **Deterministic Semantic Extraction without Generative Models**

To maintain an LLM-free architecture, the extraction phase utilizes a combination of Named Entity Recognition (NER), dependency parsing, and coreference resolution. These components work in a pipeline to transform unstructured strings into Subject-Predicate-Object (SPO) triples.9

### **Dependency Parsing and Shortest Dependency Paths**

The identification of relationships between entities is facilitated by dependency parsing, which reveals the grammatical structure of a sentence. A non-generative approach utilizes tools such as spaCy or SciSpaCy to locate the "ROOT" verb of a sentence, which typically functions as the predicate.11 For each identified entity pair, the system extracts the Shortest Dependency Path (SDP), which represents the most direct grammatical connection between the head and tail entities.12

The SDP algorithm proceeds as follows:

1. Process the input sentence ![][image1] to obtain a dependency tree where each token is a node.  
2. Identify the target entity ![][image2] and a candidate entity ![][image3].  
3. Locate the common ancestor (ROOT) in the dependency tree for both ![][image2] and ![][image3].  
4. Extract the path of tokens connecting ![][image4].  
5. If a common path exists, it is designated as the relation ![][image5]. Otherwise, the headwords of the entities are combined to form a fallback relation.12

This method ensures that every extracted relation is grounded in the syntactic rules of the language rather than the probabilistic patterns of an LLM. In biomedical relation extraction tasks, this deterministic SDP approach combined with a graph-driven framework (BioGSF) has achieved F-scores as high as 96.68%, demonstrating the efficacy of non-generative methods.12

### **Person-Centric Filtering via Coreference Resolution**

In biographical texts, the subject is frequently referenced through pronouns ("he," "she," "his") or indirect mentions. Without robust coreference resolution, the resulting knowledge graph would be fragmented, with facts scattered across disconnected nodes.10 A person-centric filtering system identifies all mentions of the target entity and resolves them to a single canonical node.8

| Subtask | Metric (F-score) | Method/Framework |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Target-Entity Mention Detection | 0.808 | OntoNotes-based Model |
| Entity-Related Event Detection | 0.859 | DistilBert-based Model |
| Inter-Annotator Agreement (Entity) | 0.65 \- 0.86 | Cohen's Kappa |
| Inter-Annotator Agreement (Event) | 0.72 \- 0.86 | Cohen's Kappa |

The use of a special "LINK" tag for copular and light verbs (e.g., "was," "is," "became") further enhances the resolution process by ensuring that state-of-being attributes are correctly associated with the person node.8 This ensures that the extracted facts—such as "was born in" or "is a writer"—are directly anchored to the subject of the biography.8

## **Topological Knowledge Representation and Embedding-Driven Grouping**

Following the extraction of isolated triples, the data is integrated into a Knowledge Graph (KG). To perform filtering and grouping without GNNs, the system utilizes Knowledge Graph Embeddings (KGE). These models map entities and relations into a low-dimensional vector space while preserving the structural properties of the graph.15

### **Translational and Semantic Matching Score Functions**

The pipeline evaluates the plausibility of each extracted triple ![][image6] using a score function ![][image7]. In translational models like TransE, a relation is treated as a translation vector in the entity space, such that the sum of the head entity vector and the relation vector should be close to the tail entity vector.18

The TransE score function is defined as:

![][image8]  
To address the limitations of TransE in modeling one-to-many or many-to-one relations (common in biographies, such as multiple awards won by one person), more advanced models like DistMult and TransH are employed.17 DistMult uses a bilinear scoring mechanism with a diagonal relation matrix ![][image9]:

![][image10]  
These scoring functions allow the pipeline to group semantically similar relations (e.g., "born in," "birth place," "native of") by placing their relation vectors in proximity within the embedding space.21 Triples with scores exceeding a certain threshold are identified as irrelevant or erroneous and are pruned from the graph before table construction.18

### **Node and Edge Similarity for Entity Resolution**

A central task in biographical table generation is the deduplication of entities and attributes. When multiple text segments refer to the same concept (e.g., "New York City" vs. "NYC"), the pipeline uses embedding similarity to merge these nodes.11

The similarity computation involves two steps:

1. **Semantic Similarity**: Calculate the cosine similarity between the word vectors (e.g., Word2Vec, GloVe, or BioWordVec) of two nodes ![][image11] and ![][image12].24  
   ![][image13]  
2. **Structural Similarity**: Evaluate the overlap of neighboring nodes in the graph structure. If two nodes share a high proportion of common neighbors with similar edge weights, they are likely identical.24  
   ![][image14]

By setting a threshold (typically ![][image15]), the system can autonomously group related facts and entities, ensuring that the final table contains normalized, non-redundant information.24

## **Multi-Hop Pruning and Flow-Based Path Selection**

One of the most persistent challenges in graph-based information foraging is the "knowledge explosion" problem, where the retrieval of multi-hop neighbors introduces significant noise that degrades the quality of the structured output.29 To maintain the person-centric nature of the data, the pipeline implements advanced pruning algorithms.

### **Pruning-hop2Token and Structural Filtering**

The MPGT-Align framework introduces "Pruning-hop2Token," a non-parametric method for neighborhood aggregation that filters out "noisy" structural information.31 This approach is particularly effective for large-scale KGs where GNN message-passing would be computationally prohibitive. The algorithm identifies a "reliable relation matching set" (![][image16]) based on the frequency and structural consistency of relations.31

When aggregating information from an entity's ![][image17]\-hop neighborhood, the system only considers paths where the connecting relations belong to ![][image16].31 This filtered adjacency matrix ![][image18] ensures that the resulting entity representations are grounded in the most salient biographical facts, such as birth dates and occupations, while ignoring peripheral associations found in broader knowledge bases like DBpedia.31

### **Flow-Based Path Pruning**

To further refine the path selection between the target entity and its attributes, the PathRAG methodology utilizes a flow-based pruning algorithm with distance awareness.29 This algorithm propagates a "resource mass" from the subject node throughout the graph. The flow ![][image19] at a given node ![][image20] at distance ![][image21] is calculated using an exponential decay function:

![][image22]  
where ![][image23] is a decay coefficient.33

Paths with a flow value below a predefined threshold are pruned. This mechanism ensures that the generated table focuses on the most direct and reliable evidence found in the text, guiding the system to generate logical and coherent summaries.29 Experimental results indicate that flow-based pruning can significantly reduce redundant information while improving the logicality of the output by up to 17%.29

## **Deterministic Schema Induction and Table Construction**

The transition from a filtered Knowledge Graph to a structured table requires a mapping between abstract graph edges and the specific fields of the target schema (e.g., the Wikipedia Template:Infobox). Traditionally, this mapping has been performed manually, but deterministic approaches like the KOG system automate this through Markov Logic Networks (MLN).35

### **Markov Logic Networks for Attribute Mapping**

KOG (Kylin Ontology Generator) casts the problem of infobox schema mapping as a joint-inference task. It treats each infobox template as a class and each slot as an attribute. By analyzing Wikipedia's edit history and string similarities, the system builds a subsumption hierarchy of classes.35

The mapping process follows these steps:

1. **Transfer Frequency**: If a set of attributes is frequently transferred between two classes (e.g., from Person to Musician), they are matched.35  
2. **Joint Inference**: An MLN is used to simultaneously predict ISA relationships (e.g., "A Scientist is a Person") and map attributes (e.g., "Researcher.born" maps to "Person.birth\_date").35  
3. **Property Inheritance**: Attributes from parent classes are automatically suggested for child classes, ensuring that the generated table follows a consistent taxonomy.35

| Class A (Source) | Class B (Target) | Mapping Condition | Mechanism |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| actor.spouse | person.spouse | High Transfer Freq | Edit History Analysis |
| horseRacers | horse\_racers | String Similarity | Case Boundary Splitting |
| scientist.born | person.birth\_date | Joint Inference | Markov Logic Network |
| writer.works | author.publications | Semantic Overlap | WordNet Mapping |

This deterministic mapping allows the pipeline to transform the raw triples extracted from the WikiBio text into a standardized format that adheres to the established Wikipedia infobox structure without the need for generative rephrasing.35

### **Deterministic Table Formatting**

The final stage of the pipeline converts the normalized triples into the specific Wiki-markup required for rendering. As specified in the Template:Infobox guidelines, this involves organizing data into optional fields, hiding headers when empty, and applying template-specific CSS classes.38 A rule-based formatter processes the final set of (Attribute, Value) pairs:

* If a field is present in the extracted KG, it is populated into the template.  
* If a field is empty, it is omitted to prevent cluttered tables.  
* Multiple values for a single field (e.g., several occupations) are joined using standard delimiters (e.g., commas or \<br /\> tags).37

## **Evaluation Frameworks and Structural Integrity Metrics**

The performance of a non-generative Text-to-KG-to-Table pipeline is evaluated using a combination of linguistic and structural metrics. While traditional NLP metrics like BLEU and ROUGE provide a baseline for string overlap, they are often insufficient for assessing the factual fidelity of structured data.4

### **The PARENT Metric for Factual Fidelity**

The PARENT (Precision and Recall of Entailed N-grams from the Table) metric was developed specifically for structure-to-text tasks and is equally applicable to the inverse.6 Unlike BLEU, which only compares the output to a reference text, PARENT accounts for the information present in the source structured data (the KG/Table).4

Experimental results on the WikiBio dataset show the following performance for various non-generative and traditional baselines:

| Model Architecture | BLEU Score | PARENT Score |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| SANA (Non-autoregressive) | 45.7 | 76.9 |
| KGPT (Knowledge-grounded) | 45.1 | 76.3 |
| Transformer (Non-pretrained) | 44.3 | 74.0 |
| UniD2T (Unified Pre-training) | 50.4 | 79.8 |

The high PARENT scores (approaching 80\) for these models indicate that the structural constraints inherent in KG-based pipelines effectively mitigate the risk of unfaithful generations.4

### **Structural Integrity of the Knowledge Graph**

Beyond the final table, the internal Knowledge Graph is evaluated for structural correctness using schema-aware metrics. These quantify the degree to which the generated graph reflects the underlying domain ontology.19

1. **Instantiated Class Ratio (ICR)**: The ratio of ontological classes (e.g., BirthPlace, AlmaMater) that are successfully instantiated in the KG based on the text.  
2. **Instantiated Property Ratio (IPR)**: The ratio of predefined properties (e.g., spouse, occupation) that are populated with valid triples.  
3. **Class Instantiation (CI)**: A weighted measure of the depth and complexity of the inheritance patterns within the generated graph.41

By monitoring the deviation between the generated KG and a "deterministic baseline KG" (built using explicit rules), the pipeline can detect "hallucinated" nodes or semantic drift before the table is finalized.41 A Hallucination Score, defined using SPARQL validation and entity tracing, provides a proactive alert system for identifying anomalies in real-time textual data streams.41

## **Addressing Bias and Social Dimensions in Biographical Data**

The transformation of biographical narratives into tables reveals critical patterns in how different social groups are represented. Research focusing on the "intersectional analysis of biographical events" in the WikiBio corpus has highlighted systemic biases.8

For example, a study of 48,789 Wikipedia biographies of writers found that personal life events (e.g., "married," "gave birth") are significantly more likely to be extracted from the biographies of women, whereas career events (e.g., "published," "won award") dominate the biographies of men.8

| Demographic Group | Frequency of Career Events | Frequency of Personal Events | F-Score (Detection) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Male Writers | High | Low | 0.859 |
| Female Writers | Low | High | 0.859 |
| Western Writers | High | Moderate | 0.808 |
| Non-Western Writers | Moderate | Low | 0.808 |

A deterministic pipeline, by explicitly categorizing and grounding each extracted fact in the KG, provides a transparent methodology for measuring these "stereotypes on salient social dimensions".44 Unlike LLMs, which may inadvertently smooth over these biases or introduce their own, a deterministic pipeline preserves the original data distribution, allowing for rigorous audit and correction of the knowledge base.14

## **Future Directions in LLM-Free Knowledge Extraction**

The evolution of Text-to-KG-to-Table pipelines is increasingly focused on improving the efficiency of graph construction and the scalability of entity alignment. Innovations like "Jigsaw-LightRAG" suggest a modular approach where the KG is decomposed into document-level subgraphs, enabling localized updates that save significant computational resources.46

Furthermore, the integration of hyperbolic embeddings (e.g., the Poincaré ball model) offers a promising alternative to Euclidean space for representing the hierarchical nature of biographical information.20 Hyperbolic spaces can capture latent hierarchical information more accurately with fewer dimensions, which is vital for modeling complex family lineages or professional hierarchies in long-form biographies.47

Ultimately, the move toward non-generative, embedding-driven extraction reflects a broader shift toward "agentic science," where AI systems are not merely tools for generation but autonomous agents capable of structured reasoning and data-driven discovery.25 By maintaining strict adherence to syntactic rules and geometric representations, these pipelines ensure that the transformation of human history into machine-readable data remains both precise and fundamentally trustworthy.

46. Jigsaw-Like Knowledge Graph Generation: A Study on Generalization Patterns with a LightRAG Implementation \- Preprints.org, accessed on March 31, 2026, [https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202511.1058/v1](https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202511.1058/v1)  
47. Hyperbolic hierarchical knowledge graph embeddings for biological entities \- PubMed, accessed on March 31, 2026, [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37778673/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37778673/)  
48. Inventing with Machines: Generative AI and the Evolving Landscape of IS Research, accessed on March 31, 2026, [https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/isre.2025.editorial.v36.n4](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/isre.2025.editorial.v36.n4)
